gn: Hakatu Royal Navy imbareteve gueteri ("Kriegsmarine") alemán-gui ha ikatu ombokapu oimeraẽve ygarata oikéva Canal de la Mancha rupive.

en: However, very few Royal Navy ships were based near the likely invasion routes as the admirals were afraid they would be sunk by German air attack.
gn: Hakatu, sa’ieterei ygarata Royal Navy mba’éva oguereko heda tee umi tape ikatuhárupi ojeike, almirante-kuéra okyhyje rupi ombokapúramog̃uarãhi’arikuéra alemán-kuéra mba’yruveve.

en: Let's start with an explanation about Italy's plans. Italy was mainly the "little brother" of Germany and Japan.
gn: Ñañepyrũ jaikuaávo mba’épa Italia ojapose ra’e. Italia ha’e upéramo Alemania ha Japón “kyvy”.

en: It had a weaker army and a weaker navy, although they had just built four new ships right before the beginning of the war.
gn: Oguerekova’ekue peteĩ ejército ha ygarata ikangyvéva, jepéramo ojapo ramoite hikuái irundy ygarata pyahu ñorãirõ guasu oñepyrũ mboyve.

en: Italy's main goals were African countries. To capture those countries, they would need to have a troop launching pad, so as troops could sail across the Mediterranean Sea and invade Africa.
gn: Italia ohupytyse umi Africa-pegua tetãnguéra. Ojapyhy hag̃uã umi tetãnguéra oguerekova’erã peteĩ plataforma tropa-kuéra, ikatuhag̃uãicha oike Mar Mediterráneo rupive ha ohekojopy Africa-pe.

en: For that, they had to get rid of British bases and ships in Egypt. Besides those actions, Italy's battleships were not supposed to do anything else.
gn: Upevarã, ohejareiva’erã hikuái umi base ha ygarata británico Egipto-pe. Avei upéva ári, umi ygarata italiano ndojapoveímiva’erã mba’eve.

en: Now for Japan. Japan was an island country, just like Britain.
gn: "Ko’agã Japón-peguarã. Japón ha’eva’ekue peteĩ tetã insular, ha’eháicha Gran Bretaña."

en: Submarines are ships designed to travel underwater, and remain there for an extended amount of time.
gn: Umi submarino hína ygarata ojejapova'ekue oiko hag̃ua oñapymĩhápe ha oikokuaa upéicha heta arange peve.

en: Submarines were used in World War I and World War II. Back then they were very slow and had a very limited shooting range.
gn: Submarino-kuéra ojeporuva’ekue Peteĩha Ñorãirõ Guasu ha mokõiha Ñorãirõ Guasu Yvy’ape’ariguápe. Upéramo guare imbegue gueteri hikuái ha nombokapúi gueteri mombyry.

en: In the beginning of the war they mostly travelled on top of the sea, but as radar began developing and becoming more accurate the submarines were forced to go under water to avoid being seen.
gn: Ñorãirõ ñepyrũme, opavave submarino oikundaha ysyry guasu ári, hakatu radar oñembokatypyryvépe, umi submarino-kuéra oñapymĩma ojehecha’ỹhag̃uãicha.

en: German submarines were called U-Boats. The Germans were very good at navigating and operating their submarines.
gn: Umi submarino alemán héra U-Boats. Alemán-kuéra ikatupyry ygaratápe jeiko ha submarino jeporúpe.

en: Because of their success with submarines, after the war Germans aren't trusted to have many of them.
gn: Oipurukuaa rupi hikuái umi submarino, ñorãirõ opa rire, oje’émi va'ekue Alemania-gua ndoguerekóiha heta submarino.

en: Yes! King Tutankhamun, sometimes referred to as "King Tut" or "The Boy King", is one of the most well known ancient Egyptian kings in modern times.
gn: Upeichaite! Rey Tutankamón, sapy’ánte ojeheróva “Rey Tut” térã “ Rey mitã’iva”, ha’e rey itujavéva Egipto-pe, ojekuaavéva ko’ag̃a rupi.

en: Interestingly, he was not considered to be very important in ancient times and was not recorded on most ancient king lists.
gn: Jaikuaahag̃uãnte, noñemomba’eguasúi chupe ymaitevéramo ha noñemboguapýi héra umi rey-kuéra réra rysýi ymave guarépe.

en: However, the discovery of his tomb in 1922 made him a celebrity. While many tombs of the past were robbed, this tomb was left virtually undisturbed.
gn: Hakatu, he’õngue renda jejuhu 1922-pe, omomba’eguasueteri ichupe. Heta te’õngue renda ymaguaréva oñemonda, kóva katu ojejuhu aimete oĩhaguéichaite.

en: Most of the items buried with Tutankhamun have been well preserved, including thousands of artefacts made from precious metals and rare stones.
gn: Aimete tembiporukuéra oñeñotyva’ekue Tutankhamon-ndive, ojejuhu porãiterei, oĩhápe hetaiterei tembiporu ojejapopyre metal-gui ha ita ojehecha’ỹva.

en: The invention of spoke wheels made Assyrian chariots lighter, faster, and better prepared to outrun soldiers and other chariots.
gn: Umi rueda ñemoheñói rupive mba’yrumỹi asirio-va ivevúi ha ipya’eve ha ohejakuaáma tapykuépe soldado-kuéra ha ambue mba’yrumỹi oĩvape.

en: Arrows from their deadly crossbows could penetrate the armor of rival soldiers. About 1000 B.C., the Assyrians introduced the first cavalry.
gn: Hu’ykuéra oipurúva ikatu ombokua umi armadura oipurúva oñorairõva hendivekuéra. Ary 1000 a.C.,- kuérape rupi umi asirio ogueroikéma kavaju jeporu ñorairõhápe.

en: A cavalry is an army that fights on horseback. The saddle had not yet been invented, so the Assyrian cavalry fought on the bare backs of their horses.
gn: Caballería ha’e peteĩ ejército oñorairõva kavaju ári. Hakatu ndoguerekói gueteri ikalcha jeguapyrã, upévare caballería asiria oñorairõ kavaju nandi ári gueteri.

en: We know many Greek politicians, scientists, and artists. Possibly the most known person of this culture is Homer, the legendary blind poet, who composed two masterpieces of Greek literature: the poems Iliad and Odyssey.
gn: Roikuaa heta político-pe, tembiakuaahára ha tembiapojeguahára griego-va. Umiva apytépe ojekuaavéva ikatu hína Homero, ñe’ẽpapahára griego, ohaiva’ekue jehaipyporãmby mba’eguasuetéva: Ñe’ẽpoty Ilíada ha Odisea.

en: Sophocles and Aristophanes are still popular playwrights and their plays are considered to be among the greatest works of world literature.
gn: Sófocles ha Aristófanes ha’e gueteri ñe’ẽpapahára popular ha hembiapokuéra oñemomba’eguasu yvy’ape’árigua jehaiporãmby iporãvéva.

en: Another famous Greek is a mathematician Pythagoras, mostly known for his famous theorem about relations of the sides of right triangles.
gn: Avei ambue grigo ojehecharamovéva ha’e matemático Pitágoras, ojekuavéva omoheñói rehe teorema umi triángulo rectángulo rehegua.

en: There are varying estimates for how many people speak Hindi. It is estimated to be between the second and fourth most commonly spoken language in the world.
gn: Hetaichagua ñeimo’ã oĩ avakuéra oñe’ẽva hindi rehe. Oje’etemi oĩha mokõiha ha Irundyha ñe’ẽ apytépe oñeñe’ẽvéva yvy ape ári.

en: The number of native speakers varies depending on whether or not very closely related dialects are counted.
gn: Ñe’ẽharaitévavoi iñambuekuaa ojehecháramo umi dialecto ojoajuetereíva ojuehe.

en: Estimates range from 340 million to 500 million speakers, and as many as 800 million people can understand the language.
gn: Umíva apytépe jajuhukuaa 340 millón ha 500 millón ñe’ẽharakuéra, ha 800 millón avakuéra rupi ikatu oikumbu ko ñe’ẽ.

en: Hindi and Urdu are similar in vocabulary but different in script; in everyday conversations, speakers of both languages can usually understand each other.
gn: Hindi ha urdu ojojogua hikuái ñe’ẽavúpe hakatu ndaupéichai ijehaípe; ñomongeta katuípe ikatu ojokupytypa oñondivekuéra.

en: Around the 15th century, northern Estonia was under great cultural influence of Germany.
gn: Siglo XV mbytérupi, Estonia norte-gua, oĩva’ekue Alemania arandupyeta poguýpe.

en: Some German monks wanted to bring God closer to the native people, so they invented the Estonian literal language.
gn: Oĩ umi monje alemán-kuéra omo’ãguiséva Ñandejára umi indígena-kuéra rehe, upévarã omoheñói hikuái estonio ñe’ẽ.

en: It was based on the German alphabet and one character "Õ/õ" was added.
gn: Oñemopyenda alemán achegety rehe ha oñemoĩ tai “Õ / õ”.

en: As time passed, many words that were borrowed from German coalesced. This was the beginning of enlightenment.
gn: Ohasavo arange, heta ñe’ẽnguéra ojeporu alemán-gui ha ojoaju ojuehe. Upéicha oñepyrũ iluminación.

en: Traditionally, the heir to the throne would go straight into the military after finishing school.
gn: Ojejapova’erãháicha, trono-pegua heredero ohova’erã ejército-pe mbo’ehao omohu’ã riremínte.

en: However, Charles went to university at Trinity College, Cambridge where he studied Anthropology and Archaeology, and later History, earning a 2:2 (a lower second class degree).
gn: Hakatu Charles oho Trinity College Mbo’ehaovusúpe, Cambridge, upépe oñemoarandu Antropología ha Arqueología-pe ha uperire Historia, ohupytyhápe3 2:2 ( jehechakuaa mokõihápegua)

en: Charles was the first member of the British Royal Family to be awarded a degree.
gn: Charles  ha’e Kuri Familia Real Británica apytégui ohupytýva peteĩ título mbo’ehaovusúpe.

en: European Turkey (eastern Thrace or Rumelia in the Balkan peninsula) includes 3% of the country.
gn: Turquía Europa-gua (Tracia oriental o Rumelia en la península de los Balcanes) ojeroike 3% tetãgui.

en: The territory of Turkey is more than 1,600 kilometres (1,000 mi) long and 800 km (500 mi) wide, with a roughly rectangular shape.
gn: Turquía yvy oguereko hetave 1.600 kilómetros (1.000 millas) ipukukue ha 800 km (500 millas) ipekue.

en: Turkey's area, including lakes, occupies 783,562 square kilometres (300,948 sq mi), of which 755,688 square kilometres (291,773 sq mi) are in south west Asia and 23,764 square kilometres (9,174 sq mi) in Europe.
gn: Turquía retepy, taha’e yno’õ, oipuru 783,562 kilómetros cuadrados (300,948 millas cuadradas),upévagui 755,688 kilómetros cuadrados (291,773 millas cuadradas) ojejuhu Asia suroeste gotyo ha 23,764 kilómetros cuadrados (9,174 millas cuadradas) oĩ Europa-pe.

en: Turkey's area makes it the world's 37th-largest country, and is about the size of Metropolitan France and the United Kingdom combined.
gn: Turquía retepy ojapo ichugui 37 hápe tetã tuichavéva yvy’ape’ári ha oguereko Francia metropolitana ha  Reino Unido oñondive tuichakue rupi.

en: Turkey is encircled by seas on three sides: the Aegean Sea to the west, the Black Sea to the north and the Mediterranean Sea to the south.
gn: Turquía rehe ojere ysyry gausu mbohapyve tenda gotyo: Egeo oĩ oeste gotyo, Negro oĩ norte gotyo ha Mediterraneo oĩ sur gotyo.

en: Luxembourg has a long history but its independence dates from 1839.
gn: Luxemburgo hembiasa pukúma, katu isãso 1839 ary guiveramo.

en: Present-day parts of Belgium were part of Luxembourg in the past but became Belgian after the 1830s Belgian Revolution.
gn: Belgica yvy pehẽ kko’agãgua ymave Luxemburgo yvy va’ekue, katu ohasa Bélgica poguýpe Revolución belga 1830 rire.

en: Luxembourg has always tried to remain a neutral country but it was occupied in both World War I and World War II by Germany.
gn: Luxemburgo akói oñeha’ã oiko hag̃ua tetã oñemombytéva apañuãime ramo, katu Ñorãirõ Guasu Peteĩ ha Mokõihápe oike Alemania poguýpe.

en: In 1957 Luxembourg became a founding member of the organization which is today known as the European Union.
gn: Ary 1957-pe, Luxemburgo oĩ atyvete ojeheróva Unión Europea omoñepyrũva apytépe.

en: The Drukgyal Dzong is a ruined fortress and Buddhist monastery in the upper part of the Paro District (in Phondey Village).
gn: Drukgyal Dzong ningo peteĩ fortaleza tapere ha peteĩ monasterio budista oĩva yvate Paro distrito-pe (Phondey Village-pe).

en: It is said that in 1649, Zhabdrung Ngawang Namgyel created the fortress to commemorate his victory against the Tibetan-Mongol forces.
gn: Oje’e 1649 ramo, Zhabdrung Ngawang Namgyel omoñepyrũ peteĩ fortaleza omomorã hag̃ua osẽre tenonde ñorãirõhára tibetano-mongoles rovake.

en: In 1951, a fire caused for only some of the relics of the Drukgyal Dzong to remain, such as the image of Zhabdrung Ngawang Namgyal.
gn: Ary 1951 ramo, tata guasu rupive opyta sa’imíntema Drukgyal Dzong reliquia, umíva apytépe Zhabdrung Ngawang Namgyal ra’ãnga.

en: After the fire, the fortress was preserved and protected, remaining to be one of Bhutan's most sensational attractions.
gn: Tata guasu rire, oñeñangareko pe fortaleza rehe, oikóvo chugui peteĩva umi mba’e ojehechasevéva Bután-pe.

en: During the 18th century Cambodia found itself squeezed between two powerful neighbors, Thailand and Vietnam.
gn: Siglo XVIII aja, Camboya ojejopynunga mokõi ijykepegua ipokatuvéva mbytépe, Tailandia ha Vietnam.

en: The Thais invaded Cambodia several times in the 18th century and in 1772 they destroyed Phnom Phen.
gn: Tailandi-gua oike Camboya yvýpe heta jey siglo XVIII aja ha 1772-pe ohundiete Phnom Phen.

en: In the last years of the 18th century the Vietnamese also invaded Cambodia.
gn: Umi arypaha siglo XVIII ryepýpe, Vietnan-gua avei oike Camboya yvýpe.

en: Eighteen percent of Venezuelans are unemployed, and most of those who are employed work in the informal economy.
gn: Dieciocho por ciento Venezuela-gua rupi oĩ hembiapo’ỹva ha hetaiteve umi hembiapóva apytépe omba’apo economía informal-pe.

en: Two thirds of Venezuelans who work do so in the service sector, nearly a quarter work in industry and a fifth work in agriculture.
gn: Dos tercio umi Venezuela-gua hembiapóva apytépe oñemongu’e sector servicio-pe, haimete peteĩ cuarta parte omba’apo industria-pe ha quinta parte agricultura-pe.

en: An important industry for Venezuelans is oil, where the country is a net exporter, even though only one percent work in the oil industry.
gn: Industria tuichavéva Venezuela-guápe g̃uarã hína petróleo, tetã oĩhápe exportador tuichávaramo, jepérõ upéicha uno por ciento añónte omba’apoindistria petrolera-pe.

en: Early in the nation's independence, Singapore Botanic Gardens' expertise helped to transform the island into a tropical Garden City.
gn: Tetã isãso ypýrõ guare, umi Jardín Botánico apo Singapúr-pe oipytyvõ pe isla-gui oiko hag̃ua peteĩ Ciudad Jardín tropical.

en: In 1981, Vanda Miss Joaquim, an orchid hybrid, was chosen as the nation's national flower.
gn: 1981 arýpe, Vanda Miss Joaquim,  híbrido de orquídeas, ojeiporavo tetã yvotýramo.

en: Every year around October nearly 1.5 million herbivores travel towards the southern plains, crossing the Mara River, from the northern hills for the rains.
gn: Opa ary oguahẽvape, jasypa rupi, amo 1,5 millones mymba ka’avo’uha oho sur gotyo umi llanura-pe, ohasávo río Mara, colina norte-pegua guive okyeteri rupi.

en: And then back to the north through the west, once again crossing the Mara river, after the rains in around April.
gn: Ha upéi ojevy jey norte gotyo oeste guio, ohasa jeývo río Mara, okypa rire jasyrundy rupi.

en: The Serengeti region contains the Serengeti National Park, the Ngorongoro Conservation Area and Maswa Game Reserve in Tanzania and the Maasai Mara National Reserve in Kenya.
gn: Serengeti región oreko Parque Nacional de Serengeti, peteĩ  Ngorongoro  Área de Conservación ha Reserva de Caza Maswa Tanzania-pe ha Reserva Nacional de Maasai Mara Kenia-pe.

en: Learning to create interactive media requires conventional and traditional skills, as well as tools mastered in interactive classes (storyboarding, audio and video editing, story telling, etc.)
gn: Medio interactivo apokuaarã tekotevẽ aporeko ojeikuaa ha ojeiporu akóiva, upéichaavei ojeguereko va’erã pojoapy ojeporukuaáva umi clase interactivo-pe (storyboard, ñehendurã ha ta’ãngamýi ñemohenda, tembiasañemombe’u ha ambue)

en: Interactive design requires that you re-assess your assumptions about media production and learn to think in a non-linear ways.
gn: Diseño interactivo oikotevẽ ejesareko jey neremimo’ã paha producción de medio rehegua rehe ha ejepokuaa ani eñamindu’u peteĩ hendáichante.

en: Interactive design requires that components of a project connect to each other, but also make sense as a separate entity.
gn: Diseño interactivo oikotevẽ umi componentepeteĩ apopyrãmegua tojoaju oñondivepa, katuavei tahi’añete peteĩ teĩ ha’ekuera haichaite.

en: The disadvantage of zoom lenses is that the focal complexity and number of lens elements required to achieve a range of focal lengths is much greater than for prime lenses.
gn: Desventaja ojejuhúva umi objetivo zoom reheveguápe ningo hína pe complejidad focal elementos objetivo reheguaretakue,  tekotevẽva ojehupyty hag̃ua peteĩ rango de distancias focales hetaiteve umi objetivo fijo-pe g̃uarãva rovake.

en: This is becoming less of an issue as lens manufacturers achieve higher standards in lens production.
gn: Kóvagui oiko apañuãi tuichaite’ỹmava umi lente apoha ojuhu rupiestándar ijyvatevévalente apópe.

en: This has allowed zoom lenses to produce pictures of a quality comparable to that achieved by lenses with fixed focal length.
gn: Kóva rupive umi obejtivo zoom rehevegua toguenohẽ ta’ãnga iporãngue ikatútava oñembojoja umi ta’ãnga oñeguenohẽva objetivo oiporúva distancia focal fija rupive rehe.

en: Another disadvantage of zoom lenses is that the maximum aperture (the speed) of the lens is usually lower.
gn: Ambue desventaja orekóva lente zoom rehevegua hína pe apertura máxima (ipya’ekue) michĩve jepi.

en: This makes inexpensive zoom lenses hard to use in low-light conditions without a flash.
gn: Kóva rupive umi objetivo zoom hepete’ỹva jeporu hasyve jepi hesakãpora’ỹhápe flash’ỹre.

en: One of the most common problems when trying to convert a movie to DVD format is the overscan.
gn: Peteĩ mba'e hasyvéva akói oñembohasa haguã ta'ãngamýi DVD-pe hina pe ijykekuéra ñeikytĩ.

en: Most televisions are made in a way to please the general public.
gn: Umi ta'angambyrykuéra ojejapo ombovy'a haguã ambue avakuérape.

en: For that reason, everything you see on the TV had the borders cut, top, bottom and sides.
gn: Upévare, opa mba'e rehecháva pe ta'angambyry pe oñekytĩ pe ijere, yvate, yvy gotyo ha ijykepe.

en: This is made to ensure that the image covers the whole screen. That is called overscan.
gn: Péva ojejapo hina ikatuhag̃uáicha pe ta'anga ryrýi ojagarrapa pe pantalla. Péva ojehero hina ijykekuéra ñeikytĩ.

en: Unfortunately, when you make a DVD, it's borders will most likely be cut too, and if the video had subtitles too close to the bottom, they won't be fully shown.
gn: Upeicha avei, ejaporõ pe DVD, ijyke kuera oñeikytĩ avei, ha pe video oguerekorõ taí kuéra pe ivype, ndaikatumo'ai ojehecha porã.

en: The traditional medieval castle has long inspired the imagination, conjuring up images of jousts, banquets and Arthurian chivalry.
gn: Umi castillo medieval ymaguare ymaiteguive oinspira umi py'amongeta, omomagiavo umi caballero kuéra, karu guasu ha caballeria Arturo ara peguare.

en: Even standing amidst thousand year-old ruins it is easy to bring to mind the sounds and smells of battles long gone, to almost hear the clatter of hooves on the cobbles and to smell the fear rising from the dungeon pits.
gn: Umi óga guasu rembyre su ary oguerekóva apytépe ndahasýi jagueru hag̃ua ñane akãme umi tyapu ha ty'akuã umi ñorãirõ oikova'ekue, ha avei ñahendúvaicha umi kavaju py ryapu tapehũ rehe ha ñahetũ pe kyhyje ry'akuã osẽva mazmorra guive.

en: But is our imagination based on reality? Why were castles built in the first place? How were they designed and built?
gn: Jepe jajepyʼamongeta osẽ umi ñande rembiasagui? Mba'ere ojejapo raka'e umi tavetã roga guasu kuéra? Mba'éichapa ra'e oje diseña ha oñemopu'ã?

en: Typical for the period, Kirby Muxloe Castle is more of a fortified house than a true castle.
gn: Yma guareichaite, Kirby Muxloe castillo ojoguave peteĩ hóga oñeñangareko hatãva peteĩ castillo tee rãngue.

en: Its large glazed windows and thin walls would not have been able to resist a determined attack for long.
gn: Hovetã tuichaicha cristal reheve ha hogyke pererĩ ndaikatumo’ãi kuri ombohovake are peteĩ ataque.

en: In the 1480s, when its construction was begun by Lord Hastings, the country was relatively peaceful and defense was only required against small bands of roving marauders.
gn: 1480 década-pe, Lord Hasting oñepyrũramo guare omopu’ã, ko tetã oiko va’ekue py’aguapýpe nunga ha tekotevẽ va’ekue oñeñangareko tapicha rekovai sapy’agua aty’i mimi rehe añónte.

en: The balance of power was a system in which European nations sought to maintain the national sovereignty of all European states.
gn: Pokatu ñembojoja ningo va’ekue peteĩ sistema tetã Europa-gua oiporúva oheka hag̃ua soberanía nacional jeguereko opaite umi tetã Europa-guápe.

en: The concept was that all European nations had to seek to prevent one nation from becoming powerful, and thus national governments often changed their alliances in order to maintain the balance.
gn: Ko’ápe ojeheka va’ekue hína opaite tetã Europa-gua toñepia’ã ani hag̃ua peteĩ tetãnte jepeve ohupyty pokatu, ha upévare, tetã rekuaikuéra akoiete omoambuéva iñirũ ani hag̃ua mba’eve oiko hendape’ỹ.

en: The War of Spanish Succession marked the first war whose central issue was the balance of power.
gn: Pe Guerra de Sucesión española omoñepyrũ peteĩha ñorairõ ha upépe ojeheka va'ekue pe pokatu rekojoja.

en: This marked an important change, as European powers would no longer have the pretext of being religious wars. Thus, the Thirty Years' War would be the last war to be labeled a religious war.
gn: Kóva omoambue peteĩ mba’e tuichaitereíva, umi Eropape ipokatuvéva ndaikatuvéitama rupi oreko mba’érepa omongu’éta ñorãirõ jeroviapy rehe. Upevare, pe Guerra de los Treinta Años ha’éta pe ipahapegua ojeherótava ñorãirõ jeroviapy rehe.

en: The temple of Artemis at Ephesus was destroyed on July 21, 356 BCE in an act of arson committed by Herostratus.
gn: Artemisa templo oĩva Éfeso-pe oñehundi va’ekue 21 jasypokõi 356 a.C. arýpe tarendy Herostrato omoñepyrũvape.

en: According to the story, his motivation was fame at any cost. The Ephesians, outraged, announced that Herostratus' name never be recorded.
gn: Hembiasakue omombe’uháicha, fama jehupyty oimeháicha pe mba’e omongyre’ỹve va’ekue chupe. Efesio-kuéra, pochýre, he’i va’ekue Hrostratus réra araka’eve ndohechamo’ãiha upéva.

en: The Greek historian Strabo later noted the name, which is how we know today. The temple was destroyed on the same night that Alexander the Great was born.
gn: Tembiasa haiha Grecia-gua Estrabón ohai tenondeve, ha’eha ko árape ojeikuaaháicha. Pe templo ojeitypa va’ekue pyhare Alejandro Magno heñóiramo guare.

en: Alexander, as king, offered to pay to rebuild the temple, but his offer was denied. Later, after Alexander died, the temple was rebuilt in 323 BCE.
gn: Alejandro, rey-ramo, oikuave’ẽ pe templo ñemopu’ã repy jehepyme’ẽ, katu oñembotove chupe. Uperire, Alejandro omano rire, pe templo oñemopu’ã jey 323 a.C. arýpe.

en: Make sure your hand is as relaxed as possible while still hitting all the notes correctly - also try not to make much extraneous motion with your fingers.
gn: Eñeha’ãke nde po topytu’u porã ikatuháicha epove aja ehóvo umi nota hekoitépe; eñeha’ã avei ani emongu’evai ne kuã.

en: This way, you will tire yourself out as little as possible. Remember there's no need to hit the keys with a lot of force for extra volume like on the piano.
gn: Upéicha sa’ive nekane’õta. Nemandu’áke natekotevẽiha ejopy hatãiterei umi tecla embopu hatãve hag̃ua piano-pe tekotevẽháicha.

en: On the accordion, to get extra volume, you use the bellows with more pressure or speed.
gn: Acordeón-pe, oñembopu hatãve hag̃ua ojejopy hatã térã pya’eve fuelle.

en: Mysticism is the pursuit of communion with, identity with, or conscious awareness of an ultimate reality, divinity, spiritual truth, or God.
gn: Pe misticismo ningo hína comunión, identidad térã conocimiento consciente peteĩ realidad ultima, divinidad, angapy añete térã Dios jeheka.

en: The believer seeks a direct experience, intuition, or insight into divine reality/the deity or dieties.
gn: Pe creyente oheka peteĩ experiencia directa, realidad divina térã umi deidad jeju térã ijehechakuaa.

en: Followers pursue certain ways of living, or practices that are intended to nurture those experiences.
gn: Ohapykuehóva oheka peteĩ hendaicha ojeikove hag̃ua térã mba’e ojejapokuaáva oñemombareteve hag̃ua umi tembiasa.

en: Mysticism can be distinguished from other forms of religious belief and worship by its emphasis on the direct personal experience of a unique state of consciousness, particularly those of a peaceful, insightful, blissful, or even ecstatic character.
gn: Misticismo ikatu ojehecha ambue hendaichagua jeroviapy ha iñemomorãramo omomba’eve rehe pe experiencia personal ojeguerekóva peteĩ estado de conciencia ipeteĩva rehe, ko’ýte umi heko py’aguapyvéva, hembihechakuaa, ivy’a pypukukuaáva rehe.

en: Sikhism is a religion from the Indian sub-continent. It originated in the Punjab region during the 15th century from a sectarian split within the Hindu tradition.
gn: Sijismo ningo peteĩ jeroviapy subcontinente indio-pegua.  Oñepyrũ Punjab región-pe siglo XV ramo tradición indu oñemboja’o rire.

en: Sikhs consider their faith to be a separate religion from Hinduism though they acknowledge its Hindu roots and traditions.
gn: Sijs-kuérape g̃uarã ijeroviapy ndaha’éi peteĩ hinduismo pehẽ, jepéramo he’i avei upégui ouhague hikuái.

en: Sikhs call their religion Gurmat, which is Punjabi for "way of the guru". The guru is a fundamental aspect of all Indian religions but in Sikhism has taken on an importance that forms the core of Sikh beliefs.
gn: Sijs-kuéra ohero ijeroviapy Gurmat-ramo, punjabi ñe’ẽme he’iséva “gurú rape”. Gurú ningo peteĩ teko oñeikotevẽtereíva India-pegua jeroviapykuérape g̃uarã, katu sijismo-pe ojeguereko pe mba’e omopyendáva sij jeroviapýramo.

en: The religion was founded in the 15th century by Guru Nanak (1469–1539). There followed in succession a further nine gurus.
gn: Ko jeroviapyomoñepyrũ va’ekue Guru Nanak siglo XV-ramo (1469-1539). Ha’e rire ou porundy ambue gurú ojoapykuéri.

en: However, in June 1956, Krushchev's promises were put to the test when riots in Poland, where workers were protesting against food shortages and wage cuts, turned into a general protest against Communism.
gn: Upéicharõ jepe, jasypoteĩ 1956 ramo, Krushchev promesa-kuéra oñeha’ã Polonia-pe oĩrõ guare mba’apoharakuéra oguerosapukáiva hesa’ipa rupi hi’upyrã ha tembiapo repy oñemboguejy rupi, upévagui upéi oñepyrũ peteĩ trotesta general comunismo rovake.

en: Although in the end, Krushchev sent in tanks to restore order, he did give way to some economic demands and agreed to appoint the popular Wladyslaw Gomulka as the new prime minister.
gn: Jepéramo ipahápe Krushchev oguerahauka tanque ojoko hag̃ua sarambi, omonĩ upépe ojejeruréva ha oñemoĩ peteĩ ñe’ẽme omohenda hag̃ua Wladyslaw Gomulka ojehayhúvape primer ministro ramo.

en: The Indus Valley Civilization was a Bronze Age civilisation in the northwest Indian subcontinent encompassing most of modern-day Pakistan and some regions in northwest India and northeast Afghanistan.
gn: Valle del Indo-pegua civilización ningo ou Edad de Bronce-gui, India noreste-pegua subcontinente ko’ág̃a oĩha Pakistán tuichakue nunga ha michĩmi India ha Afganistán noreste.

en: The civilisation flourished in the basins of the Indus River wherefore it derives its name.
gn: Ko civilización okakuaa río Indo pukukue, upégui ou héra.

en: Although some scholars speculate that since the civilisation also existed in the basins of the now dried up Sarasvati River, it should be aptly called the Indus-Sarasvati Civilization, while some call it the Harappan Civilization after Harappa, the first of its sites to be excavated in the 1920s.
gn: Jepéramo oĩ tapicha arandu he’íva pe civilizción oĩ rupi avei río Sarasvati syry, ko’ág̃a opáma pukuke, añetehápe ojehero va’erã kuri civilizción Indo-Sarasvati, oĩ aja ambue ohenóiva chupe civilización Harappa, upeicha héra rupi tenda ojejo’o ypyhague 1920 década-pe.

en: The militaristic nature of the Roman empire aided in the development of medical advances.
gn: Imperio romano reko militarista rehe oipytyvõ ojeguerojera haguã ñepohãnokuéra.

en: Doctors began to be recruited by Emperor Augustus and even formed the first Roman Medical Corps for use in the aftermath of battles.
gn: Pohãnoharakuéra oike ñepyrũ recluta-ramo emperador Augusto rupi ha avei oĩ Cuerpo Medico Roma-gua tenondetévape, ojeiporu haguã chupekuéra ñorairo rire.

en: Surgeons had knowledge of various sedatives including morphine from extracts of poppy seeds and scopolamine from herbane seeds.
gn: Cirujano-kuéra oikuaa va'ekue heta sedante, oĩ avei pype morfina oñeguenohẽva amapola ra'ỹigui ha escopolamina herbano ra'ỹigui.

en: They became proficient at amputation to save patients from gangrene as well as tourniquets and arterial clamps to stem blood flow.
gn: Ojapokuaa hikuái amputación oñangareko haguã paciente-kuérape gangrena-gui, ha avei torniquete apo ha pinza arterial-kuéra ojoko haguã tyguy rape.

en: Over multiple centuries, the Roman empire led to great gains in the field of medicine and formed much of the knowledge we know today.
gn: h-eta siglo aja, imperio Roma-gua ombohape heta mba'e jeguereko medicina-pe ha ojekuaa heta mba'e jaikuaáva ko'ã árape.

en: Pureland origami is origami with the restriction that only one fold may be done at a time, more complex folds like reverse folds are not allowed, and all folds have straightforward locations.
gn: Origami yvy tee hína origami ijapýrava ojejapokuaáva peteĩ pliegue año ohóvo, ndojehejai pliegue hasyvéva apo, pliegue ypy gotyoguáicha, ha opa pliegue oguereko tenda ndahasýiva.

en: It was developed by John Smith in the 1970s to help inexperienced folders or those with limited motor skills.
gn: Ojapo va'ekue Jonh Smith 1970 década-pe oipytyvõ haguã carpeta hembiasa'ỹva térã omyikuaa porã'ỹvape.

en: Children develop an awareness of race and racial stereotypes quite young and these racial stereotypes affect behavior.
gn: Mitãnguéra oguerojera peteĩ raza rehegua conciencia ha estereotipo raza rehegua imitã guive ha ko'ã estereotipo opoko compotamiento oguerekóva rehe.

en: For instance, children who identify with a racial minority that is stereotyped as not doing well in school tend to not do well in school once they learn about the stereotype associated with their race.
gn: Techapyrã, mitanguéra oikuaáva peteĩ raza sa'ivevaguaha orekóva estereotipo ndoikoiha mbo'ehaópe, ndoikói jepi mbo'ehaópe ágã oikuaa rire oje'éva heseguakuéra rehe.

en: MySpace is the third most popular website used in the United States and has 54 million profiles currently.
gn: MySpace hína mbohapyha web renda ojehecharamovéva ojeiporúva Estados Unidos-pe ha ogureko 54 millon perfil hina ko'agãite.

en: These websites have gotten a lot of attention, especially in the education setting.
gn: Ko'ã pagina internet pegua kuéra oreko heta jesareko, ha hetave upe tekombo'e apytepe.

en: There are positive aspects to these websites, which include, being able to easily setup a class page which can include blogs, videos, photos, and other features.
gn: Ko pagina internet oguereko heta mba'e porã, umia ha'e hina, ikatu embosako'i nde tekombo'e página kuéra ha'eva hina jehaipyre, ta'anga ryryi, ta'anga kuéra ha heta ambue mba'e.

en: This page can easily be accessed by providing just one web address, which makes it easy to remember and easy to type in for students who may have trouble using the keyboard or with spelling.
gn: Ko pagina internet pe ndahasýiete ojeike haguã, emoĩ nte vaerã pe direccion internet pegua, ha pea ndahasýi nemandu'a haguã, avei ndahasýi temimbo'e kuéra pe ohai haguã, ikatuva otopa hasy'i hina oipuru haguã ko teclado terã tai porã.

en: It can be customized to make it easy to read and also with as much or little color as desired.
gn: Ikatu oñemoĩ porãve ndahasýi haguã iñemoñe'ẽ, avei heta terã sa'ive sa'y kuéra ojeipotaháicha.

en: Attention Deficit Disorder "is a neurological syndrome whose classic defining triad of symptoms including impulsivity, distractibility, and hyperactivity or excess energy".
gn: Transtorno Déficit de atención rehegua hína "peteĩ síndrome neurpológico pe sintoma-kuéra ojeikuaávape oike hína impusividad, distracción ha hiperactividad térã energía rasa.

en: It is not a learning disability, it is a learning disorder; it "affects 3 to 5 percent of all children, perhaps as many as 2 million American children".
gn: Ndaha'éi peteĩ aprendizaje rehegua discapacidad, ha'e petei aprendizaje rehegua transtorno; "ojapyhy 3 ha 5 por ciento rupi mayma mitãnguérape, ikatu 2 millón mitã Estados Unidos-gua peve.

en: Children with ADD have a hard time focusing on things like school work, but they can concentrate on things they enjoy doing like playing games or watching their favorite cartoons or writing sentences without punctuation.
gn: Mitãnguéra orekóva TDA ndaikatúi oñatende ambue mba'e ikatúva mbo'ehaopegua tembiapo, katu ikatu oñetende ambue ojaposéva rehe, ñembosarái ñeha'ã térã ohecha ta'ãngamýi térã ohaikuaa oración omokytã'ỹre.

en: These children tend to get into a lot of trouble, because they "engage in risky behaviors, get into fights, and challenge authority" in order to stimulate their brain, since their brain can not be stimulated by normal methods.
gn: Ko'ã mitã iñapaluãi heta jepi, ojapo rupi tembiapo orekóva riesgo, "oike ñorãirõme, ha ombohovái mburuvichakuérape" omokyre'ỹ haguã iñapytu'ũ, ñapytu'ũnguéra ndaikatúi oñemokyre'ỹ jepiverõguáicha.

en: ADD affects relationships with other peers because other children can not understand why they act the way that they do or why they spell they way they do or that their maturity level is different.
gn: TDA opoko mba'éichapa ojoaju ambue hapicha ndive ambue mitã ndaika´tui oikũmby mba'érepa oreko pe comportamiento ojapóvape térã mba'érepa ojapo upéicha térã madurez rehegua madurez iñambue.

en: As the ability to obtain knowledge and to learn changed in such a way as mentioned above the base rate at which knowledge was obtained changed.
gn: Okakauavévo mba'ejekuaa ha ojeikuaávo avei iñambue upéicha oje'ehaguéicha, pe tasa mba'éicha ojeguereko kuri iñambuéma.

en: The approach to obtaining information was different. No longer did pressure reside within individual recall, but the ability to recall text became more of a focus.
gn: Pe enfoque ojeguereko haguã marandu iñambue kuri. Upe ojopýva ndaha'evéima tapicha imanu'áva rehe, katu upe jehaipy jegueromandu'a iñambue peteĩ enfoque ojeguerekóvape.

en: In essence, the Renaissance made a significant change in the approach to learning and the dissemination of knowledge.
gn: Upéicha, Renacimiento omoamue kuri mba'ejekuaa enfoque ha mba'ekuaa ñemoasarambi.

en: Unlike other primates, hominids no longer use their hands in locomotion or bearing weight or swinging through the trees.
gn: Iñambuéva ka'ikuéragui, yvypórakuéra ndoiporuvéima kuri ipo oguata haguã ha omoĩ ipohyikue oñemyatymóivo yvyramáta rehe.

en: The chimpanzee's hand and foot are similar in size and length, reflecting the hand's use for bearing weight in knuckle walking.
gn: Ka'i guasu po ha ipy ojoguáva tuichakue ha ipukuke, ohechaukáva po jeiporu oñemoĩ haguã pohyuikue jeguata haguã nudillo ári.

en: The human hand is shorter than the foot, with straighter phalanges.
gn: Yvypóra po michĩve ipygui, ha falange-kuéra ideréchove.

en: Fossil hand bones two million to three million years old reveal this shift in specialization of the hand from locomotion to manipulation.
gn: Po kanguekue fósil-kuéra orekóva mokõi térã mbohapy millón ary ohechauka mba'éichapa iñambue ohóvo po jeiporu jeguatágui mba'ejepokópe.

en: Some people believe that experiencing many artificially induced lucid dreams often enough can be very exhausting.
gn: Ambue tapicha oimo'ã oguerekóra kerayvoty ojekuaa porãva ha oñemokyre'ỹva oiko py'ỹi haguã ikatu ñanemokane'õ.

en: The main reason for this phenomenon is the result of the lucid dreams expanding the length of time between REM states.
gn: Ko fenómeno oikove hína umi tembiapokue ñanemandu'avéva oiko REM estado periodo aja.

en: With fewer REMs per night, this state in which you experience actual sleep and your body recovers becomes infrequent enough to become a problem.
gn: SA'ivéramo REM pyharépe, ko estado ojeguerekohápe jekete ha tete oñemyatyrõ oiko sa'ive ohóvo oiko haguãicha chugui peteĩ apañuái.

en: This is just as exhausting as if you were to wake up every twenty or thirty minutes and watch TV.
gn: Kóva ñanemo kane'õkuaa repayramoguáicha ohasa rire ohóvo 20 térã 30 aravo'i ha rehecháramo televisión.

en: The effect is dependent on how often your brain attempts to lucidly dream per night.
gn: Ko efecto ojaoju ñane apytu'ũ oñeha'ãvo oguereko haguã kerayvoty ojekuaa porã pyharépe.

en: Things did not go well for the Italians in North Africa almost from the start. Within a week of Italy's declaration of war on June 10, 1940, the British 11th Hussars had seized Fort Capuzzo in Libya.
gn: Mba'ekuéra ndohoi porã umi Italianokuéra pe Africa yvate gotyo oñepyrũ guive. Arapokõindy riremi Italia omoñepyrũ hague pe ñorãiro Jasypoteĩ 10, 1940 pe, umi pateĩha Hussars Britanikokuéra omongora Fuerte Capuzzo Libia pe.

en: In an ambush east of Bardia, the British captured the Italian Tenth Army's Engineer-in-Chief, General Lastucci.
gn: Peteĩ ñuháme Bardia kuarahy reike gotyo, umi Británico oity Ingeniero Mburuvicha Paha Ejército Italia-gua, General Lastucci-pe.

en: On June 28, Marshal Italo Balbo, the Governor-General of Libya and apparent heir to Mussolini, was killed by friendly fire while landing in Tobruk.
gn: Pe Jasypoteĩ 28 pe, Mburuvicha Italo Balbo, Tendota Guasu Libia pe gua ha jararã Mussoline pe, ojejuka peteĩ  angirũ jejapipe oguejy jave Tobrukpe.

en: The modern sport of fencing is played at many levels, from students learning at a university to professional and Olympic competition.
gn: Esgrima ñeha'ã ko'agãgua, oñeha'ã heta nivel-pe temimbo'e oikuaáva ohóvo mbo'ehaovusúpe guive ñeha'ã profesional ha olímpica peve.

en: The sport is primarily played in a duel format, one fencer dueling another.
gn: Ko ñoa'ãngatu oiko duelo formato-ramo, peteĩ escrima ra'ãha ambuéva rovake.

en: Golf is a game in which players use clubs to hit balls into holes.
gn: Golf hína peteĩ ñembosarái tapichakuéra oiporuhápe peteĩ yvyra ombota haguã pelota peteĩ yvykuápe.

en: Eighteen holes are played during a regular round, with players usually starting on the first hole on the course and finishing on the eighteenth.
gn: Papoapy yvykua oñeha'ã peteĩ ronda aja, ha'ãhára oñepyrũva yvykua peteĩhápe ha omohu'ã papoapyhápe.

en: The player who takes the fewest strokes, or swings of the club, to complete the course wins.
gn: Ha'ãhára ombota sa'ivéva, te´rã swings club, ojapopa haguã upe embiapo ha'evéma.

en: The game is played on grass, and the grass around the hole is mown shorter and called the green.
gn: Ñembosarái oiko kapi'ipépe upe yvykua jerére ha héra green.

en: Perhaps the most common type of tourism is what most people associate with traveling: Recreation tourism.
gn: Ikatu hína pe turismo ojeikuaavéva ha'e umi tapicha ombojoajúva jeho rehe: turismo jepytu'urãva.

en: This is when people go to a place that is very different from their regular day-to-day life to relax and have fun.
gn: Kóva oiko tapichakuéra oho jave peteĩ tendápe iambuéva oikohaguo opytu'u ha ovy'a haguã.

en: Beaches, theme parks and camp grounds are often the most common places frequented by recreational tourists.
gn: Y rembe'y ha campamento oikoha jepi umi tenda ohovehápe turista recreativo-kuéra.

en: If the objective of one’s visit to a particular place is to get to know its history and culture then this type of tourism is known as cultural tourism.
gn: Ojehupytyséva jehópe hína ojeikuaa haguã hembiasa ha cultura oguerekóva, upéicharamo upe turismo héra turismo kuaapyrã.

en: Tourists may visit different landmarks of a particular country or they may simply opt to focus on just one area.
gn: Turista-kuéra ohokuaa ambue tenda oĩva peteĩ tetã ryepýpe térã ikatu ojesareko peteĩ área añónte rehe.

en: The Colonists, seeing this activity, had also called for reinforcements.
gn: Colono-kuéra, ohechávo ko tembiapo, ojerure avei kuri pytyvõ.

en: Troops reinforcing the forward positions included the 1st and 3rd New Hampshire regiments of 200 men, under Colonels John Stark and James Reed (both later became generals).
gn: Tropa-kuéra omombarete tenda oĩhépe oike kuri 1ha ha 3ha regimiento New Hampshire-gua orekóva 200 tapicha, oĩva Coronel-kuéra John Stark ha JAmes Reed poguýpe (mokõivévagui oiko general kuri pe ka'arúpe)

en: Stark's men took positions along the fence on the north end of the Colonist's position.
gn: Stark ñorairõharakuéra oñemohenda kuri pe cerca pukukuépe pe Colono-kuéra renda norte apýra gotyo

en: When low tide opened a gap along the Mystic River along the northeast of the peninsula, they quickly extended the fence with a short stone wall to the north ending at the water's edge on a small beach.
gn: Marea baja ojapóramo petei brecha ysyry Mystic pukukuépe península norte gotyogua pukukuére, pya'e oñemyasãi cerca peteĩ ita muro rehe norte gotyo opáva y rembe'ýre peteĩ playa michĩva rehe.

en: Gridley or Stark placed a stake about 100 feet (30 m) in front of the fence and ordered that no one fire until the regulars passed it.
gn: Gridley térã Stark omoĩ kuri peteĩ estaca peteĩ 30 m(100 pies)cerca rovái ha he'i ani avave ombokapu umi regular-kuéra ohasa peve.

en: The American plan relied on launching coordinated attacks from three different directions.
gn: Estados Unidos-gua rape'apo ipyenda kuri opoívo peteĩ ataque coordinado mbohapy tenda iñambuéva guive.

en: General John Cadwalder would launch a diversionary attack against the British garrison at Bordentown, in order to block off any reinforcements.
gn: General Cadwalde opoíta kuri peteĩ ataque oporombotavýtava guarnición británica rovaque, ohapejoko haguã refuerzo-kuéra.

en: General James Ewing would take 700 militia across the river at Trenton Ferry, seize the bridge over the Assunpink Creek and prevent any enemy troops from escaping.
gn: General James Ewing ogueraha kuri 700 miliciano río trnton rupive, ojapyhýta kuri puente oĩva Assunpink Creek ári ha ohapembotýta tropa enemiga-pe ani oho hikuái.

en: The main assault force of 2,400 men would cross the river nine miles north of Trenton, and then split into two groups, one under Greene and one under Sullivan, in order to launch a pre-dawn attack.
gn: Asalto apoha fuerza orekóva 2400 ñorairõhára ohasáta kuri río 9 milla Trenton norte gotyo ha oñemboja'óta kuri mokõi hendápe, peteĩ omokãva Greene ha ambue omoakãva Sullivan, ojapo haguã peteĩ ataque ko'ẽ mboyve.

en: With the change from the quarter to the half mile run, speed becomes of much less importance and endurance becomes an absolute necessity.
gn: Oñemoambuévo peteĩ carrera orekóva un cuatro de milla-gui media milla-pe, pye'ekuee ndaha'evéima mba'eve ha resistencia katu iñambue peteĩ oñeikotevẽtévape.

en: Of course a first-class half-miler, a man who can beat two minutes, must be possessed of a fair amount of speed, but endurance must be cultivated at all hazards.
gn: Upéicha, medio kilómetro primera clase-pe, peteĩ tapicha oguahẽkuaa mokõi aravo'ípe ipya'e nunga va'erã, katu resistencia ojeguereko va'erã opaite peligro-kuérape.

en: Some cross country running during the winter, combined with gymnasium work for the upper part of the body, is the best preparation for the running season.
gn: Peteĩ ñeñani campo traviesa rehe, oñembojoapývo tembiapo gimnacio-pe tete yvate gotyoguápe, upéva oiko porãvéva hína ñeñani temporada-pe guarã.

en: Proper nutritional practices alone cannot generate elite performances, but they can significantly affect young athletes' overall wellness.
gn: Jekaru porã ijeheguíntema ndaikatúi ojepo peteĩ rendimiento yvatéva, katu opokokuaa atleta imitãva jeiko porã rehe.

en: Maintaining a healthy energy balance, practicing effective hydration habits, and understanding the various aspects of supplementation practices can help athletes improve their performance and increase their enjoyment of the sport.
gn: Eguerekóramo equilibrio energético, hey'u heta ha ojesareko heta suplementación apo rehe oipytyvõkuaáva atleta-kuérape oiko porãve haguã rendimiento orekóva ha ovy'ave haguã ojapo jave deporte.

en: Middle distance running is a relatively inexpensive sport; however, there are many misconceptions regarding the few pieces of equipment required to participate.
gn: Ñeñani orekóva media distancia hína peteĩ deporte ndahepýiva; katu oĩ heta concepto ojavýva umi equipo ojeiporu va'erãva ojejapo haguã.

en: Products can be purchased as needed, but most will have little or no real impact on performance.
gn: Mba'erepykuéra ojejoguakuaa katu hetave oguerekóta sa'i térã mboyve impacto social rendimiento orekóvape.

en: Athletes may feel that they prefer a product even when it provides no real benefits.
gn: Atleta-kuéra ikatu oñandu mba'érepa oipotave peteĩ mba'erepy jepéramonome'ẽi peteĩ beneficio oikóva.

en: The atom can be considered to be one of the fundamental building blocks of all matter.
gn: Oje'ekuaa átomo ha'eha ñemba'e'apo bloque ipyendáva upe materia-pe guarã.

en: Its a very complex entity which consists, according to a simplified Bohr model, of a central nucleus orbited by electrons, somewhat similar to planets orbiting the sun - see Figure 1.1.
gn: Peteĩ entidad orekóva complejidad ha'éva, ojesarekóvo modelo simplificado Bohr mba'éva rehe, peteĩ nucleo central ojeréva electrón-kuéra rehe, ojoguáva planeta-kuéra ojeréva kuarahýre. Ehecha Figura 1.1

en: The nucleus consists of two particles - neutrons and protons.
gn: Nucleo oguereko mokõi partícula: neutrón ha protón-kuéra.

en: Protons have a positive electric charge while neutrons have no charge. The electrons have a negative electric charge.
gn: Protón-kuéra oguereko peteĩ carga eléctrica positivo-va ha katu neutrón-kuéra ndoguerekói carga. Electrón-kuéra oguereko carga eléctrica negativo-va.

en: To Check the victim, you must first survey the scene to ensure your safety.
gn: Ojesareko haguã víctima rehe, ojesareko raẽ va'erã escena rehe oñangareko hagua ijehe.

en: You need to notice the victim's position as you approach him or her and any automatic red flags.
gn: Ojesareko va'erã mba'écihapa opyta ra'e víctimia oñembojávo hese ha oĩmeraẽ alerta ijehegui reheguáva,

en: If you get hurt trying to help, you may only serve to make matters worse.
gn: Eñehungáramo eiporopytyvõ jave, ikatu oiko embovaive haguãnte.

en: The study found that depression, fear and catastrophising mediated the relationship between pain and disability in lower back pain sufferers.
gn: Kuaapy ojuhu depresión, kyhyje ha catástrofe omombyteha tasy ha discapacidad oguerekóva umi mba'asy lumbar oguerekóvape.

en: Only the effects of catastrophizing, not depression and fear was conditional of regular weekly structured PA sessions.
gn: Umi efecto catastrófico añónte, ndahaʼéi depresión, ha kyhyje oĩ kuri consicionado umi sesión oikóva arapokõindýpe teteku'e oñembosako'íva rupive.

en: Those participating in regular activity required more support in terms of negative perception of pain distinguishing the differences of chronic pain and discomfort feel from normal physical movement.
gn: Ambue ojapóva teteku'e oikotevẽve kuri ykeko tasy percepción negativa rehe, oikumbývo mba'éichapa iñambue tasy crónico ha hasyvýva ojeheteku'e jave.

en: Vision, or the ability to see depends on visual system sensory organs or eyes.
gn: JEhecha, térã jehechakuaa, oikotevẽ tesáre térã temiandu órgano sistema visual-pegua rehe.

en: There are many different constructions of eyes, ranging in complexity depending on the requirements of the organism.
gn: Oĩ heta tesa apo, pe hasykue iñambuéva ojoehegui ojesarekóvo ambue tapicha organismo rehe.

en: The different constructions have different capabilities, are sensitive to different wave-lengths and have differing degrees of acuity, also they require different processing to make sense of the input and different numbers to work optimally.
gn: Mba'e'apo iñambuéva oguereko ambue katupyry, oñandu ambue onda pukukue ha oguereko jehecha porã iñambuéva, oikotevẽ avei peteĩ prosamiento iñambuéva ome'ẽ haguã mba'e andu jeike ha papapy iñambuévape oiko porã haguã.

en: A population is the collection of organisms of a particular species within a given geographic area.
gn: Peteĩ aty tekoha ha'e ojuehagua organísmo oĩva peteĩ especie ryepýpe, oikóva peteĩ tenda imba'eteévape.

en: When all individuals in a population are identical with regard to a particular phenotypic trait they are known as monomorphic.
gn: Opaite individuo-kuéra peteĩ atýpegua ojojoguáramo rasgo fenotípico ojueheguápe, ojekuaa monomórfico-ramo.

en: When the individuals show several variants of a particular trait they are polymorphic.
gn: Umi individuo-kuéra ohechaukávo oguerekoha hetamba'e imba'eva añónte ojehero hína polimórfico.

en: Army ant colonies march and nest in different phases as well.
gn: Tahyikuéra aty ejército oguata ha oñembohupa heta hendápe avei.

en: In the nomadic phase, army ants march at night and stop to camp during the day.
gn: Oikundaha aja tahyikuéra ejército-pegua oguata pyharekue ha opytu'u arakuépe.

en: The colony begins a nomadic phase when available food has decreased. During this phase, the colony makes temporary nests that are changed everyday.
gn: Tahyikuéra aty oñepyrũ oikundaha sa'ivévove hembi'u. Kóva oiko'aja, tahyikuéra aty oñembohupa sapy'a ha oguerova ára ha ára.

en: Each of these nomadic rampages or marches lasts for approximately 17 days.
gn: Ko mba'e oiko sapy'a ha ñeikundaha ikatu hi'are 17 ára.

en: What is a cell? The word cell comes from the Latin word "cella", meaning "small room", and it was first coined by a microscopist observing the structure of cork.
gn: Mba'épa hína peteĩ célula? Ko ñe'ẽ ou latín ñe'ẽgui "cella", he'iséva "koty michĩva", ha oipuru ñepyrũva'ekue peteĩ microcopista oma'ẽva peteĩ corcho retepýre.

en: The cell is the basic unit of all living things, and all organisms are composed of one or more cells.
gn: Célula ha'e hína opaichagua oikovéva unidad básica, ha opaite organismo-kuéra oñemohenda peteĩ térã hetave celula rehe.

en: Cells are so basic and critical to the study of life, in fact, that they are often referred to as "the building blocks of life".
gn: Celula-kuéra ha'e tekove ñepyrũmby jeikuaaverã, ojekuaavoi avei "tekove'apo ñepyrũramo".

en: The Nervous System maintains homeostasis by sending nerve impulses through out the body to keep the flow of blood going as well as undisturbed.
gn: Sistema nervioso ojoko homeostasis-pe, oguerahauka rupi impulso nervioso ava rete rupive osyryry katui hag̃ua tuguy, ojehapejoko'ỹ rehe.

en: These nerve impulses can be sent so quickly throughout the body which helps keep the body safe from any potential threat.
gn: Ko'ã impulso nervioso ikatu ojeguerahauka pya'eterei retepýre, oipytyvõva rete jeikove oimeraẽ amenaza oguerekóvagui.

en: Tornadoes strike a small area compared to other violent storms, but they can destroy everything in their path.
gn: Tornado ho'akuaa peteĩ apu'a michĩvapente ambue tormenta-kuéra hatãva rovake, hakatu ikatu omosarambi ojuhuva guive hapépe.

en: Tornadoes uproot trees, rip boards from buildings, and fling cars up into the sky. The most violent two percent of tornadoes last more than three hours.
gn: Tornado-kuéra ohapo'o yvyramáta, omboveve ogakuéra ha omombo mba'yrukuéra yvate. Dos por ciento tornado hatãvéva hi'arekuaa hetave mbohapy aravógui.

en: These monster storms have winds up to 480 km/h (133 m/s; 300 mph).
gn: Ko'ã tormenta ivaietereíva oguereko yvytu ohupytykuaáva 480 km/h (133 m/s; 300 mph).

en: Humans have been making and using lenses for magnification for thousands and thousands of years.
gn: Avakuéra ojapo ha oipuru lente jehecha tuichaverã hetaiterei arýma .

en: However, the first true telescopes were made in Europe in the late 16th century.
gn: Hakatu telescopio tee jejapo ñepyrũmby ojejapo Europa-pe siglo 16 opapotávo.

en: These telescopes used a combination of two lenses to make distant objects appear both nearer and larger.
gn: Ko'ã telescopio oipuru peteĩ combinación mokõi lente rehegua ojapo hag̃ua umi mba'e oĩ mombyrýva ojekuaa hi'ag̃ui ha tuichaiteve.

en: Greed and selfishness will always be with us and it is the nature of cooperation that when the majority benefit there will always be more to gain in the short term by acting selfishly
gn: Mba'epotarasa ha peteĩmembánte oĩkatuetéta ñande apytépe ha ñome'ẽmby ryepýpe jeiko añónte ikatúta jahupyty jeikoporãve oñondivepa.

en: Hopefully, most people will realise that their long term best option is to work together with others.
gn: Upéicha ikatu, opavave tapicha ohechakuaa iporãveha ñamba'apopáramo oñondivepa.

en: Many people dream of the day when humans can travel to another star and explore other worlds, some people wonder what's out there some belive that aliens or other life may live on another plant.
gn: Oĩ heta tapicha ikera’yvotýpe tog̃uahẽ upe ára avakuéra oikundakuaáramo ambue mbyjápe ha oikuaa ambue mundo oĩva, avei oñeporandu mba’épa oĩ upe okaháre, oĩ oimo’ãva oĩha extraterrestre térã ambue tekove oĩkuaáva ambue planeta-pe.

en: But, if this ever does happen probably won't happen for a very long time. The stars are so spread out that there are trillions of miles between stars that are "neighbors".
gn: Hakatu, kóva oikóramo, ndoiko’are pukomo’ãi. Mbyajakuéra mombyryeterei oĩ ojuehegui ha mombyryetereirasa opyta peteĩ mbyja ambue mbyjágui ha’éva “ojoykeregua”.

en: Maybe one day, your great grandchildren will be standing atop an alien world wondering about their ancient ancestors?
gn: Ikatu mba’e peteĩ árape, nde bisnieto-kuéra oĩ ambue mundo alienígena-pe ha oñeporandu iñemoñare ymaguare rehe?

en: Animals are made of many cells. They eat things and digest them inside. Most animals can move.
gn: Mymbakuéra ojejapo héta celula-gui. Ho’u mba’e oĩva ha oho hyepýpe. Oĩhaguéicha mymbakuéra ikatu omỹi.

en: Only animals have brains (though not even all animals do; jellyfish, for example, do not have brains).
gn: Mymbakuéra añónte oguereko cerebro ( hakatu ndaha’éi opaite mymba; medusa, ndoguerekói cerebro ).

en: Animals are found all over the earth. They dig in the ground, swim in the oceans, and fly in the sky.
gn: Mymbakuéra ojejuhu yvy’apu’a tuichakue javeve. Ojo’o yvykua, oyta ysyry guasúpe ha oveve arapy rehe.

en: A cell is the smallest structural and functional unit of a living (things) organism.
gn: Peteĩ célula ha’e peteĩ unidad estructural ha funcional michĩvéva peteĩ organismo oikovéva-gui (mba’e)

en: Cell comes from the Latin word cella which means small room.
gn: Cell ou latí ñe’ẽgui he’iséva koty michĩva.

en: If you look at living things under a microscope, you will see that they are made of small squares or balls.
gn: Ejesarekóramo oikovéva rehe peteĩ microscopio rupive, ehecháta ojejapoha cuadro michĩetereívagui térã apu’a’ígui.

en: Robert Hooke, a biologist from England, saw small squares in cork with a microscope.
gn: Robert Hooke, peteĩ biólogo Inglaterra-gua, ohecha cuadro michĩ’iva corcho-pe peteĩ microscopio rupive.

en: They looked like rooms. He was the first person to observe dead cells
gn: Ojogua kotýpe. Ha’eraẽvete tapicha ohecha va’ekue célula-kuéra omanóva.

en: Elements and compounds can move from one state to another and not change.
gn: Umi elemento ikatu ohasa peteĩ estado-gui ambue estado-pe iñambue’ỹrehe.

en: Nitrogen as a gas still has the same properties as liquid nitrogen. The liquid state is denser but the molecules are still the same.
gn: Upe nitrógeno gas-ramo oguereko gueteri nitrógeno líquido ndive, ojuehegua propiedad. Estado líquido ipohýive , hakatu moléculakuéra oguerekóva noñemoambuéi.

en: Water is another example. The compound water is made up of two hydrogen atoms and one oxygen atom.
gn: Y ha’e avei techapyrã. Y compuesto-va oñemohenda mokõi hidrógeno átomo ha oxígeno átomo rehe.

en: It has the same molecular structure whether it is a gas, liquid, or solid.
gn: Oguereko estructura molecular ojueheguaitéva, taha’e gaseosa, líquida ha sólida.

en: Although its physical state may change, its chemical state remains the same.
gn: Jepéramo estado físico ikatu iñambue, estado químico noñemoambue kuaái.

en: Time is something that is all around us, and affects everything we do, yet is difficult to understand.
gn: Tiempo ha’e mba’e ojere katuíva ñanderehe ha oĩ opamba’e jajapóvape, hakatu hasy ñaikumbyhag̃ua.

en: Time has been studied by religious, philosophical, and scientific scholars for thousands of years.
gn: Tiempo ojejesareko hese ymaite guivéma umi iñaranduvéva tupãoháre, filósofos ha tembikuuarekakuéra rehe hetaitereirasa arýma.

en: We experience time as a series of events passing from the future through the present to the past.
gn: Tiempo ningo jahechakuaa hína opamaba’e umi oikóva, ohasáva oikótavagui ko’ag̃a oikóva rehe ha oikomava’ekue rehe.

en: Time is also how we compare the duration (length) of events.
gn: Tiempo avei ha’e mba’éichapa ñambojoajuse umi oikóva pukukue ( ipukukue).

en: You can mark the passing of time yourself by observing the repetition of a cyclical event. A cyclical event is something that happens again and again regularly.
gn: Ndetevoi ikatu ehapykueho tiempo ohasávo, ehechakuévo upe oikóva jehecha jey jeývo. Evento cíclico ha’e hína mba’e oiko jey jey katuíva.

en: Computers today are used to manipulate pictures and videos.
gn: Ko’ag̃a rupive, umi computadora ojeporu oñemoambue hag̃ua ta’ãnga ha ta’ãngamýi.

en: Sophisticated animations can be constructed on computers, and this kind of animation is increasingly being used in television and films.
gn: Ikatu ojejapo animación ha’evéva computadorakuéra rupiva, ha kóichagua animación ojeporuva ára ha ára ta’ãngamỹi ha ta’ãngambyrýpe.

en: Music is often recorded using sophisticated computers to process and mix sounds together.
gn: Purahéi katuínte oñembohasajey computadora-kuéra ha’evéva rupive ojejapo ha ojehe’a hag̃ua pukuéra oĩva.

en: For a long time during the nineteenth and twentieth centuries, it was believed the first inhabitants of New Zealand were the Maori people, who hunted giant birds called moas.
gn: Siglo XIX ha XX ryepýpe aretereíma, oñeimo’ãva’ekue Nueva Zelanda avakuéra oĩ’ypyva’ekue, ha’e pueblo maorí, ojekáva hikuái guyrakuéra tuichaitereíva hérava moas.

en: The theory then established the idea that the Maori people migrated from Polynesia in a Great Fleet and took New Zealand from the Moriori, establishing an agricultural society.
gn: Upéi, teoría he’i upe pueblo maorí ohohague Polinesia-gui peteĩ ygarata guasúpe ha ojapyhy Nueva Zelanda, umi Moriori mba’éva, ha ojapo hikuái peteĩ ñemitỹregua aty.

en: However, new evidence suggests that the Moriori were a group of mainland Maori who migrated from New Zealand to the Chatham Islands, developing their own distinctive, peaceful culture.
gn: Hakatu, oĩ jeikuaa pyahu he’íva umi Moriori ha’ehague peteĩ maoríes aty continente-pegua ohova’ekue Nueva Zelanda-gui Islas Chatham-pe, ha omoakarapu’ã hikuái arandukuaa pyahu omomba’éva chupekuéra.

en: There was also another tribe on the Chatham islands these were Maori who migrated away from New Zealand.
gn: Avei oĩ ambue tribu islas Chatham-pe, ha’éva maoríes oúva Nueva Zelanda-gui.

en: They called themselves the Moriori there were a few skirmishes and in the end, the Moriori were wiped out
gn: Ha’ekuéravoi ojehero Moriori, oĩmimi ñorãirõ ha ipahápe, Umi Moriori ojejukapaite.

en: Individuals who had been involved for several decades helped us appreciate our strengths and passions while candidly assessing difficulties and even failures.
gn: Umi ávakuéra oĩva’ekue orendive orepytyvõ heta década romomba’eguasu hag̃ua ore mbaretekue h remiandu tee ojesaỹijo aja hikuái mba’épepa rojavy ha ore fracaso.

en: While listening to individuals share their individual, family, and organizational stories, we gained valuable insight into the past and some of the personalities who influenced for good or ill the culture of the organization.
gn: Rohendu aja tapichakuérape omombe'u hembiasa imba'ekuéra, hogaygua mba'e ha atypeguáva, heta mba'e guasu roikuaa ore ñemoñarekuéra rehegua ha ambue tapicha rerakuéra ojejava'ekue mba'e porã ha mba'e vai aty rekohápe.

en: While understanding one’s history does not assume understanding of culture, it does at least help people gain a sense of where they fall within the history of the organization.
gn: Oĩ porã jaikuaa ñane rembiasa tee, ha kóva ndaha’éi arandupy ñaikumby katueteva’erãha, hakatu oipytyvõ tapichakuérape oĩ oikuaa hag̃ua moõpa hína aty rembiasa ryepýpe.

en: While assessing the successes and becoming aware of failures, individuals and the whole of the participating persons discover more deeply the values, mission, and driving forces of the organization.
gn: Ojekuaara’ã  umi ojehupytýva ha ñañakangeta umi jajavyva’ekue rehe, individuo-kuéra ha ava’aty oikéva ohechakuaa porãve umimba’e oĩ porãva, misión ha aty omboguata ha omombaretéva.

en: In this instance, recalling previous instances of entrepreneurial behavior and resulting successes helped people to be open to new changes and new direction for the local church.
gn: Kóva ryepýpe, oñemandu’ava’erã  ambue guare ñemureko rembiasa ha mba’e osẽ   porãva’ekue oipytyvõva tapichakuérape oipykúivo tape pyahu ha mba’epyahu tupãope  g̃uarã.

en: Such success stories lessened the fears of change, while creating positive inclinations toward change in the future.
gn: Ko’ã  tembiasa osẽ   porãva’ekue rehegua ogueve ndojekyhyjevéi rupi ñemoambuégui, upéicha avei oĩ heta mba’e porã  tenondeverã  ñemoambue.

en: Convergent thinking patterns are problem solving techniques that unite different ideas or fields to find a solution.
gn: Pe ñepensa patron kuéra ojehuntava oñemoi porã haguã oĩ  vaiva ombojojapava umi idea kuéra terã mba'e kuaa ojetopa haguã peteĩ  solucion.

en: The focus of this mindset is speed, logic and accuracy, also identification of facts, reapplying existing techniques, gathering information.
gn: Ko akã jeipuru oheka hina ñepensa pua'e, logica jeipuru ha jeipuru hesakuaitepe, marandu jetopa, jeipuru mba'e kuaa oĩva ha marandu jeporeka.

en: The most important factor of this mindset is: there is only one correct answer. You only think of two answers, namely right or wrong.
gn: Pe mba'e tuichaveva ko metalidad rehegua ha'e hina: peteĩ ñembohovái añoite oĩ porã. Nde ikatu repensa hina mokõi ñembohováinte, oĩ porãva ha oĩ vaiva.

en: This type of thinking is associated with certain science or standard procedures.
gn: Pea ko ñepensa oñembojoja hina peteĩ  ciencia re terã mba'ejapo osẽ porãva.

en: People with this type of thinking have logical thinking, are able to memorize patterns, solve problems and work on scientific tests.
gn: Umi avakuéra opensava peicha oguereko hina pe hérava ñepensa logico, ha'ekuéra ikatu omoinge iñakape patronkuéra, omoĩ porã mba'e oĩ vaiva ha ikatu omba'apo umi examen cientificore avei.

en: Humans are by far the most talented species in reading the minds of others.
gn: Kóva oreipýtyvõ ha ome’ẽ oreve heta marandu ha material rohape’apo hag̃ua simulación-rã ha roikumby porãvévo umi proveso ore akãme.

en: That means we are able to successfully predict what other humans perceive, intend, believe, know or desire.
gn: Avakuéra ha’e, hetavérõ, especie ikatupyryvéva omoñe’ẽkuaáva hapicha remimo’ã.

en: Among these abilities, understanding the intention of others is crucial. It allows us to resolve possible ambiguities of physical actions.
gn: Péva he’ise ikatuha roimo’ã ambue yvypóra oñandúva, ojaposéva, oimo’ãva, oikuaáva teã oipotáva.

en: For example, if you were to see someone breaking a car window, you would probably assume he was trying to steal a stranger’s car.
gn: Techapyrã, ehecháramo ambue tapichápe ojokávo mba’yrumýi ventanilla, ikatu reimo’ã hína omondaseha hapicha oikuaa’ỹva mba’yrumýi.

en: He would need to be judged differently if he had lost his car keys and it was his own car that he was trying to break into.
gn: Ikatu Kuri ko tapicha omokañÿ imba’yrupegua llave ha oikese hína ipype. Ojehecha ambueva’erã upeicha rire.

en: MRI is based on a physics phenomenon called nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR), which was discovered in the 1930s by Felix Bloch (working at Stanford University) and Edward Purcell (from Harvard University).
gn: Resonacia magnética ha’e peteĩ física-pegua fenómeno (RMN), ojekuaa’ypyva’ekue 1930-pe Felix Block rupive ( omba’apóva Stanford Mbo’ehaovusúpe) ha Edward Purcell ( Harvard Mbo’ehaovusúpe)

en: In this resonance, magnetic field and radio waves cause atoms to give off tiny radio signals.
gn: Ko  resonancia-pe, campo magnético ha radio onda-kuéra ojapo umi átomo oguerahauka radio señal michĩetereíva.

en: In the year 1970, Raymond Damadian, a medical doctor and research scientist, discovered the basis for using magnetic resonance imaging as a tool for medical diagnosis.
gn: 1970-pe, Raymond Damadian, pohanõhára ha tembikuaahára, oipuru ñepyrũ   resonancia magnética diagnóstico ñepohãnorã.

en: Four years later a patent was granted, which was the world's first patent issued in the field of MRI.
gn: Irundy ary uperire oñeme’ẽ peteĩ patente, ha’éva patente ñepyrũ   yvy’ape’ári, oñeme’ẽva ko resonancia magnética campo-pe.

en: In 1977, Dr. Damadian completed the construction of the first “whole-body” MRI scanner, which he called the ”Indomitable”.
gn: 1977-pe, Pohanõhára. Damadian omohu’ã escáner resonancia magnética- rehegua" Rete oĩhaguéicha" apopy, ha ohero ichupe “Indomable”.

en: Asynchronous communication encourages time for reflection and reaction to others.
gn: Comunicación asincrónica omombarete arange akãgetarã ha ambue tapicha rehe ñemaña.

en: It allows students the ability to work at their own pace and control the pace of instructional information.
gn: Oheja temimbo’ekuérape omba’apóvo ritmo imba’évape ha oñangareko marandu oporohekombo’éva rehe.

en: In addition, there are fewer time restrictions with the possibility of flexible working hours. (Bremer, 1998)
gn: Upéicha avei, sa’ive ojehapejoko arange, ikatuhápe  oguereko posibilidad omba’apóvo. (Bremer, 1998)

en: The use of the Internet and the World Wide Web allows learners to have access to information at all times.
gn: Internet jeporu ha World Wide Web oheja temimbo’épe oguerekóvo marandu hekoitépe.

en: Students can also submit questions to instructors at any time of day and expect reasonably quick responses, rather than waiting until the next face-to-face meeting.
gn: Temimbo’ekuéra avei ikatu oguerahauka ñeporandu umi instructor-pe taha’e ha’éva aravópe ha oha’arõ ñembohovái ipya’éva, oha’arõ rangue ambue aty jojehechahápe.

en: The postmodern approach to learning offers the freedom from absolutes. There is no one good way to learn.
gn: Pe enfoque posmoderno ñemoarandurã ningo oporomosasoite. Ndaipóri peteĩ hendáicha oñeñemoarandu porã hag̃ua.

en: In fact, there is not one good thing to learn.Learning takes place in the experience between the learner and the knowledge presented.
gn: Añetehápe, ndaipóri mba’evete iporãva ojeikuaa hag̃ua, ñemoarandu ningo oñemboheko hína pe experiencia ohasáva temimbo’e kuaapy ojehechaukava kuápe.

en: Our current experience with all the do-it-yourself and information presenting, learning-based television shows illustrates this point.
gn: Ore experiencia rohasaáva umi TV programa omongu’éva tekombo’e ha bricolaje ha marandu jehechauka omyesakãporã kóva.

en: So many of us find ourselves watching a television show that informs us of a process or experience in which we will never participate or apply that knowledge.
gn: Heta ore apytégui roime rohecha peteĩ TV programa oremomarandúva moõ térã mba’épepa araka’eve ndaikatumo’ãiha roiporu ko kuaapy.

en: We will never overhaul a car, build a fountain in our backyard, travel to Peru to examine ancient ruins, or remodel our neighbour's house.
gn: Araka’eve noromyatyrõmo’ã peteĩ mba’yrumýi, noromopu’ãmo’ã i peteĩ fuente óga atukupépe, ndorohomo’ã Perú-pe rojesareko hag̃ua ruina ymaguare rehe térã noromyatyrõmo’ãi ore ykeregua róga.

en: Thanks to undersea fiber optic cable links to Europe and broadband satellite, Greenland is well connected with 93% of the population having internet access.
gn: Fibra óptica cable submarino ojoajúva Europa ha satélite de banda ancha rehe rupive, Groenlandia tavayguakuéra ojoaju hekoitépe interenet 93% rupi.

en: Your hotel or hosts (if staying in a guesthouse or private home) will likely have wifi or an internet connected PC, and all settlements have an internet cafe or some location with public wifi.
gn: Ikatu hína nde hotel térã nembohupáva (epytáramo peteĩ huésped róga térã oimeraẽva rógape) oguereko wifi térã peteĩ PC ojoajúva Internet rehe, ha mayma tava’i oguereko peteĩ cibercafé térã peteĩ henda oĩhápe wifi opavavépe g̃uarãva.

en: As mentioned above, though the word "Eskimo" remains acceptable in the United States, it is considered pejorative by many non-U.S. Arctic peoples, especially in Canada.
gn: He'iháicha yvateve, jepe pe jehero Esquimal oje acepta Estados Unidos pe, ojehecha hina despectivo heta avakuéra Artico pegua ndaha'éiva americano, Canada pe especialmente.

en: While you may hear the word used by Greenlandic Natives, its use should be avoided by foreigners.
gn: Jepe ehenduro ojeipuru pe ñe'ẽ  umi Nativo Groenlandia guare, pe jeipuru ndojapóivaerã ambue tapicha okavagua.

en: The native inhabitants of Greenland call themselves Inuit in Canada and Kalaalleq (plural Kalaallit), a Greenlander, in Greenland.
gn: Umi nativokuéra oikova hina Groenlandiape ojehero ijupekuéra Esquimales Canadape ha Kalaalleq (Papyeta Kalaallit), he'ise Groenlandiagua, Groenlandiape.

en: Crime, and ill-will toward foreigners in general, is virtually unknown in Greenland. Even in the towns, there are no "rough areas."
gn: Tekovai ha ensuguyreko pytaguakuéra gotyo akoiete hína peteĩ mba’e ndojeikuaáiva Groenlandia-pe. Umi tavaháre jepe ndapóri “tenda ijetu’uha”.

en: Cold weather is perhaps the only real danger the unprepared will face.
gn: Arapytu ro’ysã añónte hína pe peligro ikatúva ombohovái umi noñembosako’íriva upevarã.

en: If you visit Greenland during cold seasons (considering that the further north you go, the colder it will be), it is essential to bring warm enough clothing.
gn: Ehóramo Groenlandia-pe araro’y aja (ojehechávo ehovévo norte gotyo ndero’yvetaha), tekotevẽta egueraha ao haku porãva.

en: The very long days in the summer can lead to problems getting sufficient sleep and associated health issues.
gn: Arakue ipukúva arahakuaja ikatu nañanemongeporãi ha ñanembohasy.

en: During the summer, also watch out for the Nordic mosquitoes. Although they do not transmit any diseases, they can be irritating.
gn: Arahaku aja, eñangareko va’erã ñati’ũ Nordico uperupigua rehe. Nombohasáiramo jepe mba’eveichagua mba’asy, ikatu ñandejopi rasy.

en: While San Francisco's economy is linked to it being a world-class tourist attraction, its economy is diversified.
gn: Jepéramo San Francisco viru reko oĩ peteĩ atracción turística de clase mundial-ramo, iviru reko oku’e heta mba’e rupive.

en: The largest employment sectors are professional services, government, finance, trade, and tourism.
gn: Tembiapo oku’evéva hína umi servicio profesional, tetã rekuáipe, viru reko, ñemureko ha turismo.

en: Its frequent portrayal in music, films, literature and popular culture has helped make the city and its landmarks known throughout the world.
gn: Oku’e akóiva purahéi, cine, literatura ha tavaygua rekópe oipytyvõ táva ha umi henda ojeikuaavéva herakuã porã hag̃ua arapy tuichakue.

en: San Francisco has developed a large tourist infrastructure with numerous hotels, restaurants, and top-notch convention facilities.
gn: San Francisco omopu’ã peteĩ infraestructura turística tuicháva heta hotel, restaurante ha instalaciones convención de primer nivel aporã.

en: San Francisco is also one of the best places in the nation for other Asian cuisine: Korean, Thai, Indian, and Japanese.
gn: San Francisco avei tenda iporãveéva tetãmbýpe ojejuhu hag̃ua Asia rembi’u: Corea, Tailandia, India ha Japón-gua.

en: Traveling to Walt Disney World represents a major pilgrimage for many American families.
gn: Walt Disney World-pe jeho ningo omboheko peteĩ peregrinación tuicha heta familia Estados Unidos-guápe g̃uarã.

en: The "typical" visit involves flying into Orlando International Airport, busing to an on-site Disney hotel, spending about a week without leaving Disney property, and returning home.
gn: Visita “jepiveguápe” oĩ Aeropuerto Internacional de Orlando-pe jeveve, autobús-pe jeho peteĩ hotel Disney-pegua peve, ehasa rãngue peteĩ arapokõindy rupi esẽkuaa’ỹ hag̃ua Disney propiedad-gui ejevy jey hag̃ua nde rógape.

en: There are infinite variations possible, but this remains what most people mean when they talk of "going to Disney World".
gn: Umi mba’e ojejapokuaáva ndaijapýrai, katu upéva hína pe mba’e umi tapicha ohecharamovéva oñeñe’ẽ jave “Disney World-pejeho rehe”.

en: Many tickets sold online through auction websites such as eBay or Craigslist are partially used multi-day park-hopper tickets.
gn: Heta umi jeikerã oñemoñemúva web renda subasta aporã rupive, taha’e eBay térã Craigslist umíva, ikatu ojeiporu heta árape g̃uarã.

en: While this is a very common activity, it is prohibited by Disney: the tickets are non-transferable.
gn: Jepérõ kóva hína peteĩ mba’e oiko py’ỹiva, Disney ombotove: Umi boleteo ndaikatúi oñembohsa peteĩ tapichágui ambuépe.

en: Any camping below the rim in Grand Canyon requires a backcountry permit.
gn: Ambue campamento ojejapova pe Gran Cañon rembe'ype oikoteve hina permiso jehasa haguã pe tetãre.

en: Permits are limited to protect the canyon, and become available on the 1st day of the month, four months prior to the start month.
gn: Permiso kuéra oime limitado hina oñeñangareko haguã pe cañon re ha oime disponible hina pe ara peteîha pe jasy ñepyrûvo, irundy jasy oñepyrû mboyve pe jasy.

en: Thus, a backcountry permit for any start date in May becomes available on 1 Jan.
gn: Upévare, pe permiso jehasa haguã pe tetãre oñepyrûva hina Jasypo pe oñeme'ẽ hina 1 Jasyteĩ-pe.

en: Space for the most popular areas, such as the Bright Angel Campground adjacent to Phantom Ranch, generally fill up by the requests received on first date they are opened to reservations.
gn: Tenda kuéra umi tenda ojeikuaa porãitereiva, umia ha'e hina Campamento Bright Angel oimeva hina Phantom Ranch ykere, katueterei henymba pe solicitud pe ara peteîha pe ojeipe'a ove reserva ra.

en: There are a limited number permits reserved for walk-in requests available on a first come, first served basis.
gn: Oime hina papaha limitado permiso ra opytava umi pedido jeguatara itenondeveape guara.

en: Entering Southern Africa by car is an amazing way to see all the region's beauty as well as to get to places off the normal tourist routes.
gn: Africa sur-pe jeike mba’yrumýime rupive ndaijojahái ojehecha hag̃ua umi mba’eporãite uperupigua, upéicha avei oñeguahẽ hag̃ua umi ruta turística noñeguahẽguasuihápe.

en: This can be done in a normal car with careful planning but a 4x4 is highly advised and many locations are only accessible with a high wheel base 4x4.
gn: Kóva ikatu ojejapo oimehaichagua mba’yrumýime ha ñangarekopeporã, katu ojejerure tojeiporu peteĩ 4x4 de distancia entre ejes alta.

en: Keep in mind while planning that although Southern Africa is stable not all neighboring countries are.
gn: Embosako’ívo, ehechakuaa va’erã, jepéramo África sur gotyo oñeñangaekoporã, tetãnguéra ijykeregua nda’upéichai.

en: Visa requirements and costs vary from nation to nation and are affected by the country you come from.
gn: Visa jejerure repy ha oñeikotevẽva upevarã iñambue peteĩ tetãme ambue hendapeguágui ha he’iséta hína mba’e tetãguipa ojeju.

en: Each country also has unique laws requiring what emergency items need to be in the car.
gn: Mayma tetã ipeteĩ léi oiporúvape he’íva mba’e mba’épa umi tembiporu pytyvõ pya’erãoñeikotevẽva peteĩ  mba’yrumýime.

en: Victoria Falls is a town in the western portion of Zimbabwe, across the border from Livingstone, Zambia, and near Botswana.
gn: Victoria Falls hína peteĩ táva parte occidental Zimbawe-pe, ojehesara rire Livingstone, Zambia rembe’y ha Botswana ypýpe.

en: The town lies immediately next to the falls, and they are the major attraction, but this popular tourist destination offers both adventure seekers and sightseers plenty of opportunities for a longer stay.
gn: Pe táva oĩ cataratas ojehechasevéva uperupigua rembe’ýpe, katu ko destino turístico ojehoseteha  oikuave’ẽ aventura rekaha ha turista-kuérape juruja ojepyta areve hag̃ua.

en: In the rainy season (November to March), the water volume will be higher and the Falls will be more dramatic.
gn: Ára oky hetaveha jave (jasypateĩ guive jasyapy peve), y hetavéta ha umi cataratas tuichavéta.

en: You are guaranteed to get wet if you cross the bridge or walk along the trails winding near the Falls.
gn: Katuete nde rykuéta ehasakuévo puente térã eguatáramo umi tapepo’i catarata-kuéra ypyrupigua rehe.

en: On the other hand, it is precisely because the volume of water is so high that your viewing of the actual Falls will be obscured—by all the water!
gn: Katu avei, y heta rupi ndaikatumo’ãi ehecha hekoitépe mba’éichapa añetete umi catarata, y hetaiterei rupi!

en: Tomb of Tutankhamun (KV62). KV62 may be the most famous of the tombs in the Valley, the scene of Howard Carter's 1922 discovery of the almost intact royal burial of the young king.
gn: Tutankhamon tyvyty (KV62). KV62 ikatu hína tyvyty ojeikuaavéva Valle-pe, upépe Howard Carter ojuhu 1922 ramo rey pyahu tyvyty haimete mba’eve oñembyai’ỹre.

en: Compared to most of the other royal tombs, however, the tomb of Tutankhamun is barely worth visiting, being much smaller and with limited decoration.
gn: Upéicharamo jepe, tumba real ambue rovake, Tutankamón tyvyty haimete ndoikoi ojeheo hag̃ua oñema’ẽnte jepe, michĩeterei ha ndojeguaporãi rupi.

en: Anyone interested in seeing evidence of the damage to the mummy done during attempts to remove it from the coffin will be disappointed as only the head and shoulders are visible.
gn: Oimeraẽ tapicha ohechaséva mba’éichapa oñembyai momia oñeñeha’ãnguévo oñeguenohẽ hag̃ua ataúd-gui, opytáta ohechasépe,  iñakã ha ijati’ỹ pevénte ikatu rupi ojehecha.

en: The fabulous riches of the tomb are no longer in it, but have been removed to the Egyptian Museum in Cairo.
gn: Umi riqueza imomorãmbývava oĩva’ekue tumba-pe naiporivéima ipype, ojeguerahapava’ekue Museo Egipcio de El Cairo-pe.

en: Visitors with limited time would be best to spend their time elsewhere.
gn: Tapicha ohoséva sapy’aiterãnte, iporãvéta tohónte ambue hendápe.

en: Phnom Krom, 12 km southwest of Siem Reap. This hilltop temple was built at the end of the 9th century, during the reign of King Yasovarman.
gn: Phnom Krom, opytáva 12 km suroeste gotyo Siem Reap-pe. Ko templo oĩva peteĩ colina ru’ãme oñemopu’ã va’ekue siglo IX opávo, rey Yasovarman omanda aja.

en: The gloomy atmosphere of the temple and the view over the Tonle Sap lake make the climb to the hill worthwhile.
gn: Atmósfera lúgubre orekóva ko templo ha lago Tonle Sap ojekuaaporã rehe upégui, ndaha’éi mba’evete pe colina yvate jejupi.

en: A visit to the site can be conveniently combined with a boat trip to the lake.
gn: Ko tendápe jeho aja ikatu avei ojeho peteĩ barco-pe pe lago rupive.

en: The Angkor Pass is needed to enter the temple so do not forget to bring your passport along when heading to Tonle Sap.
gn: Angkor Pass katuete oñeikotevẽ ojeike hag̃ua pe templo-pe, upévare nanderesaráiri va’erã nde pasaporte-gui ehojave Tonle Sap-pe.

en: Jerusalem is the capital and largest city of Israel, though most other countries and the United Nations do not recognize it as Israel's capital.
gn: Jerusalén hina Israel Táva tee ha tuichavéva, jepéramo tetãnguéra ha Tetãnguéra Joaju Atyvete ndohechái chupe Israel táva teéramo.

en: The ancient city in the Judean Hills has a fascinating history spanning thousands of years.
gn: Ko táva tujaite oĩva Judea colína-pe hembiasa rorymi oúma miles de año guivéma.
